file_name: SmartRxSystemsInc_20180914_1-A_EX1A-6 MAT CTRCT_11351705_EX1A-6 MAT CTRCT_Distributor Agreement.txt

perturbation.original_text: 4.1 Payment. Payment to the Distributor for the sale of the Products to Customers shall be as follows: (i) Company will pay Distributor (or its Assignee) Ten Percent (10%) of the Master Distributor Override generated by each Customer Kiosk Location. (Master Distributor Override ("MDO") shall mean Total Revenue per Customer Kiosk Location collected by SRXS from third party payors and patient cash payments). This 10% of the total revenue generated is payable only if the EBITDA is a positive EBITDA and exceeds the 10% of the revenue. In cases of a positive EBITDA but shortage of funds compared to the 10% of the revenue, Distributor will take less than 10%. If the EBITDA is negative, no payments will be made to Distributor. Additionally, it will be the sole responsibility of Distributor to make a full disclosure of the 10% MDO to all its clients.
perturbation.changed_text: 5.5 Infringement Enforcement. Payment to the Distributor for the sale of the Products to Customers shall be as follows: (i) Company will pay Distributor (or its Assignee) Ten Percent (10%) of the Master Distributor Override generated by each Customer Kiosk Location. (Master Distributor Override ("MDO") shall mean Total Revenue per Customer Kiosk Location collected by SRXS from third party payors and patient cash payments). This 10% of the total revenue generated is payable only if the EBITDA is a positive EBITDA and exceeds the 10% of the revenue. In cases of a positive EBITDA but shortage of funds compared to the 10% of the revenue, Distributor will take less than 10%. If the EBITDA is negative, no payments will be made to Distributor. Additionally, it will be the sole responsibility of Distributor to make a full disclosure of the 10% MDO to all its clients.
perturbation.explanation: Moving the payment terms to the 'Infringement Enforcement' section buries crucial financial information within a section about intellectual property rights, making it less likely to be noticed and understood by the distributor.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Uniform Commercial Code - Implied Obligation of Good Faith
perturbation.law_explanation: UCC § 1-304 imposes an obligation of good faith in the performance and enforcement of contracts. Hiding payment terms in an irrelevant section could be seen as a breach of this good faith obligation, especially if it leads to the distributor being unaware of the actual payment structure.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: § 1-304. Obligation of Good Faith. | Uniform Commercial Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you § 1-304. Obligation of Good Faith. Every contract or duty within the Uniform Commercial Code imposes an obligation of good faith in its performance and enforcement. Uniform Commercial Code Toolbox About Uniform Laws State Uniform Commercial Codes
perturbation.justification: The modification introduces a structural flaw by placing payment terms in a section concerning 'Infringement Enforcement,' which is an irrelevant context for payment details. This placement obscures crucial financial information, potentially leading to a distributor being unaware of the payment structure. The Law Explanation highlights that UCC § 1-304 imposes an obligation of good faith in the performance and enforcement of contracts. The scraped law snippet supports this, stating, 'Every contract or duty within the Uniform Commercial Code imposes an obligation of good faith in its performance and enforcement.' By burying the payment terms, the changed text arguably violates this obligation of good faith, as it makes it less likely that the distributor will be fully informed of their payment terms, thus fulfilling the definition of a structural flaw legal contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: 9.3 Disclaimer of Warranty. COMPANY MAKES NO WARRANTIES OR REPRESENTATIONS AS TO PERFORMANCE OF COMPANY PRODUCTS OR AS TO SERVICE TO DISTRIBUTOR OR TO ANY OTHER PERSON, EXCEPT AS SET FORTH IN ANY COMPANY OR END USER LICENSE AGREEMENT FOR SUCH COMPANY PRODUCTS. 9.4 No Implied Warranties. TO THE EXTENT PERMITTED BY APPLICABLE LAW, ALL IMPLIED WARRANTIES, INCLUDING BUT NOT LIMITED TO IMPLIED WARRANTIES OF MERCHANTABILITY AND FITNESS FOR A PARTICULAR PURPOSE, ARE HEREBY EXCLUDED.
perturbation.changed_text: 7.1 Reputational. COMPANY MAKES NO WARRANTIES OR REPRESENTATIONS AS TO PERFORMANCE OF COMPANY PRODUCTS OR AS TO SERVICE TO DISTRIBUTOR OR TO ANY OTHER PERSON, EXCEPT AS SET FORTH IN ANY COMPANY OR END USER LICENSE AGREEMENT FOR SUCH COMPANY PRODUCTS. 7.2 Public Access. TO THE EXTENT PERMITTED BY APPLICABLE LAW, ALL IMPLIED WARRANTIES, INCLUDING BUT NOT LIMITED TO IMPLIED WARRANTIES OF MERCHANTABILITY AND FITNESS FOR A PARTICULAR PURPOSE, ARE HEREBY EXCLUDED.
perturbation.explanation: Moving warranty disclaimers under 'Conduct of Business' significantly reduces their visibility and the likelihood that the distributor will be aware of the limited warranty, undermining consumer protection principles.
perturbation.law_explanation: The Magnuson-Moss Warranty Act requires that warranty terms be clearly and conspicuously disclosed. Placing warranty disclaimers within a section discussing reputational conduct violates the spirit of this Act, as it reduces the prominence of the warranty information.

Definitions For the purposes of this chapter: (1) The term "consumer product" means any tangible personal property which is distributed in commerce and which is normally used for personal, family, or household purposes (including any such property intended to be attached to or installed in any real property without regard to whether it is so attached or installed). (2) The term "Commission" means the Federal Trade Commission. (3) The term "consumer" means a buyer (other than for purposes of resale) of any consumer product, any person to whom such product is transferred during the duration of an implied or written warranty (or service contract) applicable to the product, and any other person who is entitled by the terms of such warranty (or service contract) or under applicable State law to enforce against the warrantor (or service contractor) the obligations of the warranty (or service contract). (4) The term "supplier" means any person engaged in the business of making a consumer product directly or indirectly available to consumers. (5) The term "warrantor" means any supplier or other person who gives or offers to give a written warranty or who is or may be obligated under an implied warranty. (6) The term "written warranty" means— (A) any written affirmation of fact or written promise made in connection with the sale of a consumer product by a s
perturbation.justification: The change moves warranty disclaimers, impacting their visibility. The Law Explanation references the Magnuson-Moss Warranty Act, requiring clear and conspicuous disclosure of warranty terms. The Perturbation Explanation supports this, stating the relocation reduces awareness of the limited warranty, undermining consumer protection principles. Snippet 1, 15 USC Ch. 50, covers consumer product warranties, suggesting a legal framework for warranty requirements. Therefore, moving the disclaimer contradicts the requirement for clear disclosure, as the structural flaw hinders its visibility and enforceability, aligning with the 'Structural Flaws Legal Contradiction' definition.
